coredata.dc:title: A novel optical fiber sensor based on AIEgens for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe 3+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haibo
Tu, Qiqi
Zheng, Yong
Yan, Jiangyang
Huang, Xuguang
Meng, Hongyun
Tan, Chunhua
coredata.dc:description: A novel optical fiber sensor for the detection of Fe3+ based on AIEgens was reported for the first time. A new anti-B18H22 derivative ((5-NH2C9H6N)2B18H20, Referred as to S1) bearing 5-aminoisoquinoline was synthesized via a one-pot and its structure was verified by 1H NMR and HRMS, photophysical properties were characterized by UV–Vis, fluorescence spectrum and theoretical calculation. The fluorophore exhibits obvious aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) characteristics in THF/H2O solution by increasing the aqueous concentration from 70% to 90%. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecule in THF/H2O (ƒw = 90%) solution shows high selectivity for detecting Fe3+ over other metal ions by forming a 1:1 metal-to-ligand complex, and the detection limit is 5.25 × 10−5 M. The optical fiber sensor based on S1 for the detection of Fe3+ was fabricated, and the detection limit of Fe3+ can reach to be 0.137 nM. The sensor can be used to detect Fe3+ in chemical, environmental and biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: (5-NH2C9H6N)2B18H20
Aggregation induced emission
Optical fiber sensor
Anti-B18H22

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra measurements
Fiber optical sensor for detecting Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optical fiber sensing structure and the specific light transmission process of the sensor.
(a)Normalized emission spectrum of S1 in different solutions, c = 5 × 10−4 M, λex = 320 nm. (b)The energy level and electron density distribution of HOMO and LUMO of S1.

Normalized absorption (black line) and emission spectrum (wine red line) of S1 in THF solution, c = 5 × 10−4 M, λex = 320 nm (Inset:S1 in solid state, photographs of samples taken in daylight and under 365 nm UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The emission spectra of S1 in THF/H2O mixed solution. (b) The effect of water fraction on the emission intensity, c = 5 × 10−4 M, λex = 320 nm. (c) CIE 1931 coordinates of S1. Colors are applied according to their fluorescence emission spectra. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescent photos of S1 in THF/H2O (ƒw = 90%) solutions upon the addition of different metal ions. (b) Binding ratio of S1 and Fe3+. (c) Emission spectra of S1 with different concentration of Fe3+(0–100 equiv).
Potential complex mechanism diagram of the continuous response of S1 to detect Fe3+.
Anti-interference experiment of S1 for detecting Fe3+.
The fluorescence spectra of S1 at different pH in THF/H2O (ƒw = 90%) solutions and pH-dependence of S1.
SEM images of film on the optical fiber sensor(a)before and(b)after in pH = 1 solution(c)after in pH = 14 solution.
(a) The spectrogram of the optical fiber sensor in Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations (2 × 10−9 M-10 × 10−9 M), (b)Linear fitting of redshift distance versus Fe3+ concentration.
Comparison of the detection limits obtained by some fluorescence sensors and our work.

head.ce:title: A novel optical fiber sensor based on AIEgens for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe 3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices, School for Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University, 510006, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices
School for Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices, School for Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University, 510006 Guangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel optical fiber sensor for the detection of Fe3+ based on AIEgens was reported for the first time. A new anti-B18H22 derivative ((5-NH2C9H6N)2B18H20, Referred as to S1) bearing 5-aminoisoquinoline was synthesized via a one-pot and its structure was verified by 1H NMR and HRMS, photophysical properties were characterized by UV–Vis, fluorescence spectrum and theoretical calculation. The fluorophore exhibits obvious aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) characteristics in THF/H2O solution by increasing the aqueous concentration from 70% to 90%. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecule in THF/H2O (ƒw = 90%) solution shows high selectivity for detecting Fe3+ over other metal ions by forming a 1:1 metal-to-ligand complex, and the detection limit is 5.25 × 10−5 M. The optical fiber sensor based on S1 for the detection of Fe3+ was fabricated, and the detection limit of Fe3+ can reach to be 0.137 nM. The sensor can be used to detect Fe3+ in chemical, environmental and biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion recognition is an important issue in the field of analytical chemistry because of its wide range of applications in environmental, chemical, biological and clinical aspects [1,2]. Iron, as an essential trace element in the human body, is an important part of hemoglobin, heme, various enzymes and immune system, iron deficiency can lead to serious health problems such as anemia, kidney and liver damages [3]. On the other hand, overexposure to iron can lead to serious diseases such as organ dysfunction, Alzheimer's disease and hepatitis [4]. In addition, Fe3+ is involved in many biological phenomena such as chlorophyll synthesis, nitrate reduction and oxygen metabolism [5], and Fe3+ is a key factor affecting the growth of phytoplankton in water [6]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of iron ions is of great significance to biology and human health, it is particularly important to develop chemical sensors that can specifically identify, efficiently, accurately, quickly and portable detection of Fe3+. As is well-known, the electrochemical methods, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) have been used to detect iron ions, these analytical techniques can provide a good limit of detection (LOD) and wide linear ranges, but require expensive equipments, cumbersome sample handling and specialized personnel to operate. In recent years, fluorescence sensors have attracted wide attention in the field of biological detection due to their high sensitivity, rapidity and simplicity. Fluorescence sensing materials play an important role in the field of ion detection [7,8]. At present, some fluorescence materials for the detection of Fe3+ have been reported such as Rhodamine, BODIPY, quantum dot, MOF, etc. However, these fluorescence sensors still have some problems, such as poor water solubility of materials, high toxicity, and some of them can only be used in a single solvent [9–13].

The new concept of AIE, which is the opposite of the conventional aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), was first introduced by Tang in 2001. AIE molecules exhibit weak or non-emission in solution but strong emission upon aggregation [14]. AIE molecules have become a hot topic in the field of fluorescence since 2001, the main AIE mechanism caused by the restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM) has been accepted [15]. Hitherto, various AIE molecules have been designed and successfully synthesized for a wide range of applications in different fields, such as fluorescent materials, electronic communication and biological imaging [16–18]. AIE-based sensors for ions have developed rapidly by virtue of its fast responsiveness, superb sensitivity and simple operation [19,20].

A few fluorescent probes for Fe3+ have been achieved through the introduction of functional groups into AIE molecules, such as tetraphenylethylene derivatives [21,22], 7, 11-diphenyl-8H- acenaphth [1,2-f] isoindole-8, 10 (9H) - dione functionalized polysiloxane (PMDF) [23], thiophene functionalized silicon-cored compounds [24], cationic pillar [5]arene (CWP5) [25], pentacenequinone derivative [26], 4,4′-fluoresceinoxy bisphthalonitrile [27], biomass-based polymer nanoparticles [28], pyrene and anthracene-based schiff base derivatives [29], glutathione modified carbon-dots [30], pyrene-phosphonate conjugate [31], etc.

Trans-octadecaborane (anti-B18H22) is formed by combining two B10H14 to form two nested fusion cage structures and is of great interest as the only known binary borohydride compound that fluoresces. Due to the blue fluorescence emitted by anti-B18H22, it not only has good photostability, high quantum yield and light tunable, but also breaks the limitations of solution-treated conventional blue laser gain materials such as organic compounds and semiconductor nanocrystals in terms of solubility, chemical stability, photostability and expensive [32,33]. So far, the use of trans-octadecylborane derivatives for the detection of metal ions in aqueous solutions has not been reported. Recently, our group synthesized a new anti-B18H22 derivative through the introduction of the isoquinoline group into anti-B18H22 molecule, it exhibited good AIE property in THF/H2O mixed solution [34]. On the basis of the strong affinity of the amino group, some fluorescent probes have been designed and synthesized for the detection of the metal ions by introducing an amino group.

Based on the above considerations, 5-amino isoquinoline was elected to react with anti-B18H22, we synthesized a new anti-B18H22 derivative (S1) bearing amino group for the detection of Fe3+. The structure of compound S1 determined by 1H NMR, optical properties of S1 were characterized by photoluminescence spectrum and theoretical calculation. Compound S1 exhibited aggregation-induced emission enhancement in THF/H2O solution. It was used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ and showed higher sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, a new optical sensor for Fe3+ based on the AIEgens has been developed, the structure and principle of the sensor were described in detail. The detection performance of the sensor was also studied.

The 11B NMR and 1H NMR spectra were performed on a Bruker AVANCE NEONMR spectrometer. HRMS were carried out on a Bruker solanX 70 FT-MS spectrometer. The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 (National Thermoelectric Nico) spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were carried out on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (the slit width for excitation and emission was 10 nm and the voltage was 700 V). UV–Vis spectra were determined on a Shimadzu UV 2700 spectrophotometer. Images of sensing film were taken by a Zeiss Gemini 500 scanning electron microscope. The fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were measured using an Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. The signals of the optical fiber sensor was collected on an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA, ANDO AQ6370) (amplified spontaneous emission (ASE), wavelength range:1520 nm–1620 nm), the programmable temperature controller (JieXin Testing Equipment Co., Ltd., J-TOPH-22-B) was used.
Benzene, toluene, and methanol were all dried before use. All other commercial sources of chemicals and reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification.
The stock solution was prepared by dissolving the requisite amount of samples in a different solvent or ultrapure water.
All the calculations were carried out by using the Gaussian 09 program package and the geometries of the molecules were optimized by DFT calculations at the B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) level.

As shown in Fig. 1 , S1 was prepared by the reaction of anti-B18H22 with 5-aminoisoquinoline. Anti-B18H22 was synthesized according to the literatures [35,36]. 5-aminoisoquinoline (0.520 g, 3.607 mmol) and anti-B18H22 (0.201 g, 0.923 mmol) were dissolved in benzene solution (80 mL) and then refluxed by heating under nitrogen atmosphere for 12 h. Finally, the wine-red solution and orange-yellow precipitate were obtained. The mixture solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation under reduced pressure, and then purified by silica gel chromatography (eluent: dichloromethane/n-hexane/ethyl acetate = 5:1:1, v/v), and the second band was further purified by TLC climbing board (developing solvent: dichloromethane/n-hexane = 4:1, v/v), then a deep red solid was obtained. The eluate was purified again by column chromatography, and extracted with n-hexane, a red solid product (S1) was obtained. 1H NMR (600 MHz, CD2Cl2) δ 9.36 (s, 1H), 9.17 (s, 1H), 8.56 (s, 1H), 8.48 (s, 1H), 8.29 (dd, J = 30.9, 11.1 Hz, 1H), 7.83 (s, 1H), 7.59 (s, 2H), 7.44–7.39 (m, 1H), 7.18 (s, 1H), 7.09 (s, 1H), 6.96 (s, 1H), 6.69 (s, 1H), 6.57 (s, 1H), 5.34 (s, 6H), 4.51 (s, 2H), 4.24 (s, 1H), 4.12 (s, 1H), 3.62 (s, 2H), 3.30 (s, 1H), 2.84 (s, 2H), 2.59 (s, 4H), 2.21 (s, 1H), 2.05 (s, 4H), 1.71 (s, 18H), 1.43 (s, 4H), 1.30 (s, 10H), 1.02 (s, 2H), 0.89 (s, 6H), 0.22 (s, 1H), 0.08 (s, 1H), −0.01 (s, 1H), −0.64 (s, 1H), −2.80 (s, 1H), −3.56 (s, 1H). 11B NMR (193 MHz, CD2Cl2) δ 14.98 (s), 12.76 (s), 10.10 (s), 3.98 (s), 3.31 (s), −6.24 (s), −7.81 (s), −10.32 (s), −13.59 (s), −16.82 (s), −21.90 (s), −25.53 (s), −33.52 (s), −34.62 (s), −36.02 (s), −39.10 (s), −41.13 (s), −45.01 (s). FTIR (KBr)ν: 3468.87, 3381.77, 3207.17, 2516.58, 1625.48, 1593.43, 1499.54, 1398.12, 1146.47, 1080.28, 996.78 cm−1. HRMS (ESI m/z) for S1(B18C18H36N4): found: 503.4766 [M+H]+, calcd: 503.4724.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of S1 were prepared in THF. The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence measurements were obtained in THF. Fluorescence measurements were carried out at excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm (λabsmax(ε) = 389 nm (5 × 10−4 mol L−1 cm−1), λem = 680 nm).

The structure diagram of the sensor and the specific transmission process of the light beam are shown in Fig. 2 . A broad band light source is used to produce incident light, the temperature is controlled by a programmable temperature controller, the output optical signals are collected by a optical spectrum analyzer, the types of fiber used includes single mode fiber (SMF) and no-core fiber (NCF), the connection sequence of optical fibers is SMF, NCF, SMF, NCF, SMF.

Amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) emitted light passed through the sensor placed in a programmable temperature controller (PTC) and finally reached the optical spectrum analyzer (OSA). When the light from the ASE source reached the single-mode through the splitting effect of the first NCF which acts as a beam splitter, part of the light propagated in the single-mode cladding and most of the light still propagated in the core of the single-mode. These two beams produced interference phenomena in the second NCF and then transmitted to the single-mode fiber.

When light passed through the sensing structure, Mach-Zehnder interference occurred, resulting in a series of interference wave peaks. The theoretical derivation is as follows:
(1)
I
o u t
=
I
c a l d
+
I
c o r e
+ 2
I
c a l d
I
c o r e
cos
Δ φ
(2)
Δ φ =
2 π
(
n
e f f
c o r e
−
n
e f f
c a l d
)
L
λ
where
I
o u t
is output light intensity,
I
c a l d
is light intensity passing through the cladding,
I
c o r e
is light intensity passing through the core,
Δ φ
is the difference in light intensity between the two beams,
λ
is the wavelength of input light, L is the transmission length of the light,
n
e f f
c o r e
is the effective refractive index of the core and
n
e f f
c a l d
is the effective refractive index of the cladding after coating.

When
Δ φ =
( 2 m + 1 )
π
, there will be total interference.
(3)
λ m
=
2
(
n
e f f
c o r e
−
n
e f f
c a l d
)
L
2 m + 1
where
m
is interference series,
λ m
is the wavelength of interference peak.

The cladding of the optical fiber used to detection iron ions was etched by HF for 20 min for coating film. In order to evenly coat the optical fiber with a sensing film, firstly, digged out a long and thin slot on the polytetrafluoroethylene plate to accommodate the optical fiber, then placed the sensing structure into the slot. 300 mg of PMMA was dissolved in 10 mL chloroform in a beaker, stirred for 1 h at room temperature to obtain 30 mg/mL mixed solution, added S1(2.5 mg) into 2 mL above solution and sonicated for 10min to obtain a homogeneous solution. A dropper used to inject a little solution into the groove, rotated the optical fiber slowly at a constant speed. Finally placed it at room temperature in the dark for half an hour.

The structure of S1 is shown in Fig. 3 . The structure of S1 was confirmed by 11B NMR, 1H NMR, HRMS data and FT-IR. To investigate the relationship between the optical properties and electronic structure of S1, quantum chemical calculations (DFT/B3LYP/6-31 + G(d)) were used to study the highest occupied orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) of S1. The construction of donor-acceptor (D−A) structure and extension of π-conjugation are the commonly used strategies to shift the emission of luminophores to the red AIEgens [37]. The optical range of a molecule is determined by the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Therefore, rising HOMO or lowering LUMO is the essence of designing long-wavelength materials. Introducing electron donor (D) and acceptor (A) groups into a π-conjugation system is an approach to reduce bandgap. Based on a classic red emissive D–A fluorophore 5-aminoisoquinoline [38], connecting a neutral AIE core to the D or A part of the molecule can yield new red emissive AIEgens. To unveil the underlying mechanism of S1, we studied its photophysical behavior in solvents with different polarities to reveal whether the restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM) and twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) effect exist, as shown in Fig. 4 (a), with increasing the solvent polarity, the fluorescence emission maximum of S1 had a red shift from 647 nm to 685 nm, showing the RIM and TICT effect obviously [39]. The shape of the frontier molecular orbital is shown in Fig. 4 (b). The HOMO energy level and the LUMO energy level of S1 are calculated to be −4.51eV and −2.30eV, respectively, giving a band gap of 2.21eV. The HOMO mainly locates on the borane cluster and delocalizes around nested and reticulated subclusters and the LUMO mostly lies within the two 5-aminoisoquinoline ligands. The orbital distribution of HOMO and LUMO of S1 can explain the electron transfer mechanism between the borane clusters and the 5-aminoisoquinoline ligand, which is considered as an “inside-out” transfer. In addition, the electron density distribution of the HOMO and LUMO is indicative of a π–π transition with a degree of charge transfer character. The AIEE characteristics of S1 can be explained by the above theoretical calculation.

S1 is a reddish-brown powder under a 365 nm UV lamp in solid state (Fig. 5 ), which is associated with the fluorescence emission in the aggregate state, indicating that S1 has AIE property. Fig. 5 shows the normalized absorption and emission spectra of S1 in THF solution. It can be seen that S1 exhibits two emission peaks characterized by a weak and sharp emission peak at 450 nm followed by a strong and broad emission peak at 680 nm. The emission peak at 450 nm originates from the emission of the locally excited state fluorescence and the fluorescence emission peak at 680 nm has a large Stokes shift and a broad emission band, which is coming from the emission of the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). The maximum absorption peak of the absorption spectrum of S1 in THF solution is at 389 nm, which can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the whole molecular conjugate system, the shoulder peak at 308 nm may be related to the lone electron pair on the amino group and the n-π* transition of the large π bond of the conjugate system.

The AIE properties of S1 in THF (good solvent)/H2O (poor solvent) mixture were studied by changing the water content from 0 to 99% according to the common method for verifying the AIE properties [40]. The results are shown in Fig. 6 , it can be noticed that the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution of S1 have two emission peaks at 450 nm and 680 nm, which are similar to the spectra of pure THF solvent. The fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm does not change much during the whole dilution process. Therefore, we mainly discuss the emission peak at 680 nm. When the water content in the mixed solution increases from 0 to 70%, the fluorescence intensity of S1 at 680 nm does not change significantly. As ƒw increases from 70% to 90%, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases and reaches a maximum at ƒw = 90%, with a fluorescence intensity 6.5 times higher than that in pure THF solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of water content. The results indicate that S1 has the property of aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE).

The luminescence mechanism of this AIE molecule can be explained as follows: Based on a classic red emissive D–A fluorophore 5-aminoisoquinoline, connecting with anti-B18H22 can yield new red emissive AIEgens. Because the restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM) and twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) effect exist, the S1 has the property of aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). On the other hand, the combination of two 5-aminoisoquinoline molecules makes S1 to be a larger non-planar rigid structure. As the water content increases to 70%, nanoaggregates start to form in the mixed solution, the intramolecular motion through the B–N bond of S1 is inhibited. Meanwhile, π stacking that leads to fluorescence quenching is prevented by the non-planar rigid structure. Both of these factors lead to the suppression of non-radiative relaxation and AIE enhancement.

The effect of various metal ions on the PL spectra of S1 were investigated. The high selectivity of S1 to detect Fe3+ was further observed in the fluorescent spectra. Considering that S1 has the strongest fluorescence in a THF/H2O (ƒw = 90%) mixed solution (Referred to as S2), S2 was used to explore the effect of metal ions on the PL spectra of S1. By adding and diffusing 10 equivalents of different cations (Ba2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Zn2+, Li+, Cu2+, Fe2+, 1 × 10−3 M) to the solution, it is interesting to note that only the adding of Fe3+ solution induces fluorescence quenching, while the other metal ions do not show similar response phenomena (Fig. 7 (a)).

To further investigate the interaction between Fe3+ and S1, the fluorescence titration experiments were performed. With the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of S2 gradually weakened. As the Fe3+ concentration reached 10 times equivalent, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 81.8%, which indicated a strong correlation interaction between Fe3+ and S1. This phenomenon might be attributed to the coordination interaction between the amino group of S1 and Fe3+ [41,42]. A binding analysis using the Job-plots equation determined that the S1–Fe3+ stoichiometry was 1:1, and the detection limits (Fig. 7) and the association constant were calculated to be 5.25 × 10−5 M and 1.25 × 10−3 M. Therefore, the following fluorescence mechanism of S1 affected by Fe3+ is proposed. In general, most iron ion fluorescence sensors show a “turn-off” fluorescent phenomenon due to the intrinsic paramagnetic properties of Fe3+ [43,44]. In this case, the “turn-off” fluorescence properties of S1 may be caused by the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. After adding Fe3+ in the S2 solution of S1, the suppression of the PET is destroyed and the fluorescence is quenched [45] (Fig. 8 ).

Acid-base stimulus-responsive luminescent material [46] is a typical fluorescent sensing material. To investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence spectrum of S1, the pH titration diagram of S1 in S2 solution is shown in Fig. 10 . As can be seen in Figs. 10 and S1 exhibits intense luminescence at pH ≤ 4. The increase in fluorescence intensity is due to the induced electron transfer (PET) that inhibits the protonation of the heteroatoms in S1, which leads to a radiative relaxation process of excited molecules. The fluorescence intensity does not vary much from pH 5 to 10. In a strong base (pH > 10) environment, the fluorescence at 680 nm is quenched. The mechanism is that strong bases induce the deprotonation of heteroatoms in S1 to form a lone pair of electrons for photo-induced electron transfer (PET), leading to the non-radiative relaxation of the molecule [47]. In addition, the deprotonation of the amino group also leads to the disruption of the compound, which is responsible for the fluorescence quenching of S1. Due to the weakness of the hydrogen bonding force, nanoscale holes appears on the surface of the fiber optic sensor as shown in Fig. 11 (a). After putting the sensors in pH = 1 solution and pH = 14 solution separately, the aperture decreases in pH = 1 solution and the holes are “closed” in pH = 14 solution as shown in Fig. 11(b and c). The results show that S1 can be “closed” by strong alkali in S2 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fiber optical sensor for detecting Fe3+

ce:section.ce:para: A new anti-B18H22-based derivative S1 was synthesized and characterized. Compound S1 exhibited significant aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) properties in THF/H2O solution with an increase in aqueous solution concentration from 70% to 90%, and exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ over other metal ions in S2 solution. The performence for detecting Fe3+ of S1 was satisfactory in linear work with a detection limit of 5.25 × 10−5 M. Moreover, compared with fluorescence methods that also use a fluorescence quenching mechanism to detect Fe3+, this new S1-based optical fiber sensing structure was developed to improve the detection limit to be 0.137 nM. The sensor has the advantages of high selectivity, high detection limit, easy operation and remote monitoring, with potential applications in chemical, environmental and biological detection fields.

Haibo Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration. Qiqi Tu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yong Zheng: Validation. Jiangyang Yan: Validation. Xuguang Huang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hongyun Meng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chunhua Tan: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel approach towards crown-ether modified polyimides with affinity for alkali metal ions recognition
